title: THE SIGNIFICANCE OF MONOCYTE CCR2-CCL2 AXIS IN TRIPLE NEGATIVE BREAST CANCER
author: Budzyń, Magdalena
license_note: Zenodo record metadata license field: cc-by-4.0
description:
Background:    The monocyte CCR2-CCL2 axis plays a crucial role in forming tumor-associated macrophages (TAMs), subsequently promoting tumor metastasis and resistance to therapy.  
   Aims:    Therefore, our study assessed the monocyte CCR2-CCL2 axis in triple-negative breast cancer (TNBC) and its ability to predict tumor response to neoadjuvant chemotherapy (NAC).  
   Methods:    A study included forty-two female patients diagnosed with TNBC and qualified for NAC. Responses to neoadjuvant chemotherapy were based on the pathological complete response (pCR). Surface expression of CCR2 on monocytes was evaluated by flow cytometry. Circulating CCL2 was measured by Luminex X-Map technology.   
   Results:    Increased monocyte CCR2 expression and higher circulating CCL2 levels were observed in the entire group of TNBC patients.     After dividing the patients according to their response to NAC, a significant difference was only found in the CCL2 concentration between patients who achieved and did not achieve pCR. ROC curves showed that the optimum diagnostic cut-off value of CCL2 ≤ 89.61 pg/mL better discriminates TNBC patients who achieved pCR than the Ki-67 index. Univariate analysis demonstrated that circulating CCL2 ≤ 89.61 pg/mL was significantly associated with pCR. However, in the multivariate model, this correlation lost statistical significance.   
   Conclusions   : Our study demonstrated the activation of the monocyte CCR2-CCL2 axis in TNBC for the first time. This activation occurs mainly in patients who do not respond to NAC. Circulating CCL2 levels ≤ 89.61 pg/mL were found to predict, to some extent, the achievement of pCR in TNBC patients receiving NAC.
